attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman is undergoing elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anaesthesia. She is otherwise healthy and well, with no significant medical history and no regular medications. Thirty minutes into the procedure, during surgical manipulation of the gallbladder, the anaesthetist notices her pulse rate increases suddenly from 65/min to 115/min, blood pressure from 120/80 mmHg to 165/95 mmHg, and respiratory rate from 12/min to 22/min. There are no other changes in monitored parameters.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for these changes?
attributes.explanation:
This patient demonstrates an acute autonomic response during surgery, with increases in heart rate, blood pressure and respiratory rate. These changes occurred in response to surgical stimulation, which is highly suggestive of inadequate anaesthetic depth.

Inadequate depth of anaesthesia typically presents with signs of sympathetic activation including tachycardia, hypertension, and tachypnoea in response to surgical stimuli. Other signs may include sweating, lacrimation, and movement. Immediate recognition and treatment by deepening the anaesthetic is essential to prevent awareness and potential psychological trauma.

Acute blood loss would typically present with hypotension and tachycardia as compensatory mechanisms, not hypertension. Additionally, it would be unusual for physiological changes to occur so acutely from surgical blood loss.

Pneumoperitoneum from laparoscopic insufflation can cause some haemodynamic changes, but these typically occur at the time of insufflation. Vagal stretch can result in bradycardia, and increased intra-abdominal pressure may compress abdominal vessels and impair venous return, decreasing cardiac output.

Anaphylaxis during anaesthesia typically presents with bronchospasm, hypotension, and cutaneous signs. The presence of hypertension rather than hypotension makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Air embolus typically presents with sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypotension, and a decrease in end-tidal carbon dioxide. The presence of hypertension rather than hypotension makes this diagnosis unlikely.
learningPoints: Tachycardia, hypertension and tachypnoea during surgery may indicate inadequate depth of anaesthesia or awareness.
Movement, lacrimation, and sweating are other signs of inadequate anaesthesia or awareness.
Immediate recognition and deepening of anaesthesia is essential to prevent psychological trauma from awareness.
references: Bullard TL, Cobb K, Flynn DN. Intraoperative and Anesthesia Awareness. [Updated 2023 Jan 29]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK582138/
M. M. Ghoneim, Richard B. Weiskopf; Awareness during Anesthesia. Anesthesiology 2000; 92:597 doi: https://doi.org/10.1097/00000542-200002000-00043

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
